drills.scenario: Maria runs a Shopify pet brand (organic dog treats). She has a Meta Pixel, a list of 6,000 past customers with email + phone + names, 18,000 newsletter leads, and a Facebook page with 9,000 fans. She wants to scale to brand-new, cold buyers who look like her best customers.
drills.question: What is the single highest-leverage way to use her assets to find quality COLD buyers?
options: Boost posts to her 9,000 page fans repeatedly until they buy again
Build a lookalike audience seeded from her 6,000-customer list, ideally weighted by Customer Lifetime Value
Target broad interests like 'dogs' and 'pets' with no warm-audience input
Upload the customer list as a Custom Audience and run conversion ads to it
drills.explanation: Lookalikes are the most powerful way to find cold audiences: combine YOUR data with Facebook's. Seeding from the 6,000-customer list (above the ~1,000 minimum) and adding LTV as a data field builds lookalikes off her highest-value customers, not just any customer. Fans and the uploaded customer list are WARM audiences, not cold scaling.

drills.scenario: A local CrossFit gym in Austin wants more members. The owner copies the interest-research playbook used by an ecommerce friend and starts brainstorming 20 interests like 'CrossFit Games,' 'Reebok,' and 'Rich Froning.'
drills.question: What's the core targeting mistake here, per the module?
options: 20 interests is too few for a gym; local businesses need ~50
Local businesses are primarily GEO-targeted, not interest-based; she should drop a pin / set a city radius
She should use a 1% lookalike instead of interests
Gyms can't be advertised on Meta at all
drills.explanation: The BCOP interest-research process is explicitly for ecommerce, digital, and information products — NOT local businesses, which use geographic targeting (type a city with a 10-50 mi radius, or drop a pin with a 1-50 mi radius). A local gym should target by location, not stack fitness interests.

drills.scenario: A B2B coach builds one ad set targeting people interested in 'small business' AND who are homeowners AND parents. He stacks all three interests into the single detailed-targeting 'include' box, expecting Meta to require all three. His audience comes back at 14 million people instead of the tight niche he wanted.
drills.question: What happened, and how should he fix it?
options: Meta defaults to AND; he should lower his budget to shrink reach
Stacking in one include box is OR targeting (matches at least one); he must use audience Narrowing to get AND
Nothing is wrong; 14 million is the correct narrow audience
He should add more interests to the same box to tighten it
drills.explanation: Stacking criteria in one 'include' box is OR targeting — people qualify by meeting at least ONE criterion, which inflates reach. To require ALL conditions (small business AND homeowner AND parent) he must use the Narrow feature, which creates AND logic. This is the classic 'OR-by-accident' pitfall.

drills.scenario: Devon sells an online course and wants to build the abandoned-cart retargeting audience so he can win back people who added to cart but didn't buy. He's in Ads Manager ready to create the audience.
drills.question: Put the steps to build the abandoned-cart Custom Audience in the correct order.
options: Go to Audiences and Create Custom Audience
Choose Website Traffic as the source
Select Custom Combination so you can use event rules
Switch the dropdown from URL to Event
INCLUDE the AddToCart event
EXCLUDE the Purchase event
drills.explanation: The abandoned-cart audience is built as a Website Traffic custom audience using Custom Combination, then switching the rule dropdown from URL to Event, INCLUDING AddToCart and EXCLUDING Purchase. This isolates people who started but did not complete checkout — the hot audience worth recovering.

drills.scenario: A skincare brand's winning ad set targets interest 'Drunk Elephant' and is profitable at a low CPA. The media buyer wants to scale by finding MORE audiences similar to this winner using Audience Scaling (Tier 2 of the Five-Tier Scaling System). She finds 4 new relevant, properly-sized interests.
drills.question: What is the correct next move with her winning ad set?
options: Add all 4 new interests into the existing winning ad set's include box
Duplicate the winning ad set and insert each new interest into a separate duplicate, keeping the Post ID for social proof
Delete the original and rebuild from scratch with the 4 interests
Raise the budget on the original ad set by 300% overnight
drills.explanation: Five-Tier Scaling, Tier 2 Audience Scaling: after identifying new relevant interests, Duplicate the winning ad set and insert new interests into the duplicates (one per duplicate so you can compare per-interest performance). Reuse the Post ID to carry over accumulated social proof. Piling all interests into one ad set destroys your ability to compare response per interest.

drills.scenario: A coach selling a $497 webinar-to-course funnel has only 220 customers but 4,800 email leads. She wants to build a lookalike but worries her customer count is too small to seed a good one.
drills.question: What's the best move given the data she has?
options: She can't build any lookalike until she reaches exactly 1,000 customers
Step up the funnel and seed the lookalike from her 4,800 leads, testing it against the customer-based one
Skip lookalikes entirely and only run interest targeting
Scrape competitor followers to pad her source list to 1,000
drills.explanation: When you lack ~1,000 customers, step UP the funnel and use leads as the lookalike source — the 4,800 leads clear the minimum and give Facebook more data (trading quality for quantity). Test lead-based vs. customer-based to see which wins. Lookalikes can work from ~1,000 data points; scraping violates Facebook policy.

drills.scenario: A boutique hotel on the coast wants to fill rooms. The marketer sets up geo targeting around the hotel and must choose a location refinement mode. The goal is to reach tourists who are more than 125 miles from home and physically in the area right now.
drills.question: Which location refinement mode fits this goal?
options: People who live in this location
Everyone in this location (the default)
People traveling in this location
People recently in this location
drills.explanation: 'People traveling in this location' is defined as users more than 125 miles from home — ideal for travel and tourism. 'Recently in this location' uses phone location for local outreach, 'live in' targets residents, and the default 'Everyone in this location' captures anyone currently in the area regardless of travel status.

drills.scenario: During an Audience Insights research session for a fishing-gear store, a buyer finds the interest 'Roland Martin Outdoors' under Page Likes. Excited, he records it and plans to launch an ad set on it tomorrow.
drills.question: What must he do before relying on that interest, per BCOP?
options: Nothing — anything shown in Audience Insights is automatically targetable
Confirm the interest actually exists as a targetable interest in Power Editor and record its reach
Build a lookalike from it immediately
Combine it with 10 other interests in one ad set
drills.explanation: BCOP Step 2 (Confirm): an interest shown in Audience Insights may NOT exist as a targetable interest in Power Editor. You must verify it and record its potential reach before building on it. 'Audience Insights does not equal targetable' is an explicit pitfall.

drills.scenario: A media buyer for a digital-products brand is setting up a Website Conversions campaign and wants to use exclusion targeting correctly to protect ad spend and keep audiences genuinely cold or efficient.
drills.question: Which exclusions are sound, recommended practices? (Select all that apply.)
options: Exclude existing leads/customers on a conversion campaign so you don't pay to re-acquire them
Exclude your page fans when you specifically want to reach a truly cold audience
Exclude everyone who has ever visited your site, even on retargeting campaigns
Exclude geos or interests that consistently waste spend
drills.explanation: Exclusions protect ad spend: exclude existing leads/customers on conversion campaigns, exclude fans to reach a truly cold audience, and exclude wasteful geos/interests. But excluding ALL past site visitors on a RETARGETING campaign is wrong — retargeting depends on reaching those warm visitors, so option 3 defeats the purpose.

drills.scenario: A US apparel brand wants to use the 'large audience' algorithm play (targeting a 4-5M audience and letting Meta optimize) on its Website Conversions ad sets. Currently each ad set drives about 8 conversions per week.
drills.question: What does the module say about whether this will work?
options: It will work fine; conversion volume is irrelevant to large-audience targeting
The large-audience play needs at least 20-30 conversions/week per ad set; at 8/week it will fail until volume rises
Large audiences only work for local businesses
She should shrink the audience to under 100K to fix it
drills.explanation: The large-audience strategy (4-5M, best case 40-50M) only works ABOVE a conversion-volume floor: 20-30 conversions/week per ad set minimum on the Website Conversions objective. At 8/week she's below the floor and the algorithm lacks signal. For smaller-population countries the fallback is a 100K-500K audience.
